title: Four Travelers Enter the City
subtitle: Four Witnesses to the City of Victory
figure: Paes, Nunes, Abdur Razzaq and Nicolo de' Conti
region: Vijayanagara, Calicut, Goa, Persia and Italy's travel circuits
moral: Listen to eyewitnesses, but test every dazzling number.

sources.citation: Nicolo de' Conti in Poggio Bracciolini, De Varietate Fortunae, c. 1447
sources.note: Early European description of the city around 1420.

sources.citation: Abdur Razzaq, Matla-us-Sadain, 1443, trans. Elliot and Dowson
Paes and Nunes in Robert Sewell, A Forgotten Empire (1900)
sources.note: Portuguese accounts from Krishnadevaraya and Achyuta Deva Raya's time.

panels.shot: epic wide shot from a rocky hill above Vijayanagara

dialogue.text: From Italy, Persia and Portugal, four travelers reached Vijayanagara between 1420 and 1537, leaving words that still glow with the dust and splendor of the City of Victory.

panels.shot: medium shot montage of four faces separated by visual panels

dialogue.text: Nicolo de’ Conti around 1420, Abdur Razzaq in 1443, Domingo Paes in 1520, and Fernao Nunes in the 1530s—each saw Vijayanagara in a different reign, yet spoke of the same vast city.

panels.shot: sweeping bird’s-eye over Indian Ocean routes

dialogue.text: In the fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, ocean winds and caravan roads carried merchants, ambassadors and exiles toward a rising power in the south—Vijayanagara, the City of Victory.

panels.shot: slow push-in on Nicolo de’ Conti walking a dusty road

dialogue.text: Nicolo de’ Conti, an Italian who wandered twenty-five years in the East, reached the great southern city around 1420, long before Europeans spoke of Calicut and Goa as they later would.

panels.shot: wide shot of early Vijayanagara from a hill at sunrise

dialogue.text: Conti saw a large, rich city with many temples and strong walls, ruled by a Hindu king whose power stretched far across the south—an early glimpse of Vijayanagara’s growing might.

panels.shot: interior close medium of Conti speaking to Poggio Bracciolini

dialogue.text: Years later, his memories flowed into Latin through Poggio Bracciolini’s pen, in De Varietate Fortunae, turning his wandering into one of Europe’s first descriptions of Vijayanagara.

panels.shot: majestic low-angle of Abdur Razzaq at a bustling Persian port

dialogue.text: In 1443, Abdur Razzaq, envoy of the Timurid court, sailed from Persia to India, bearing letters and expectations, charged to measure the strength of this distant infidel king.

panels.shot: wide shot of Vijayanagara’s royal street as Razzaq arrives

dialogue.text: Razzaq entered Deva Raya II’s court and was struck by its order: long streets, bazaars, and fortified enclosures, a capital vast enough to rival cities he knew in Persia.

panels.shot: medium interior of Razzaq in audience hall

dialogue.text: He wrote of Deva Raya’s wealth and courtesies, of elephants, cavalry and infantry in numbers almost beyond belief, praising the king’s discipline and the scale of his armies.

panels.shot: close-up of Razzaq writing by lamplight

dialogue.text: In Matla-us-Sadain he cast Vijayanagara in Persianate mirrors—mixing fact with literary flourish, counting troops in tens of thousands, arranging the city in the familiar language of empire.

panels.shot: dynamic wide of Krishnadevaraya’s Vijayanagara with markets and temple towers

dialogue.text: Nearly eighty years later, Domingo Paes reached Krishnadevaraya’s court around 1520, finding the city at its zenith—rich markets, soaring gopuras, and a ruler famed for war and poetry.

panels.shot: low-angle wide of the royal stables and horse traders

dialogue.text: Paes saw what mattered to him most: horses. He described endless stables and brisk trade, for he had his own interests in the business that tied Goa’s ports to this inland power.

panels.shot: epic wide of Mahanavami festival platform

dialogue.text: He marveled at the Mahanavami festival, where Krishnadevaraya reviewed armies on towering platforms, turned the city into a theatre of power, and made ritual and military spectacle one.

panels.shot: close-up of Paes watching the king conduct affairs

dialogue.text: In his report, Paes praised the king’s justice and diligence, describing daily audiences and careful listening, a monarch who balanced war with administration and ritual with routine.

panels.shot: medium shot of Fernao Nunes at a desk with Paes’s manuscript

dialogue.text: In the 1530s, Fernao Nunes, a later Portuguese observer, gathered Paes’s account and his own impressions, adding detail and numbers, shaping a narrative of Vijayanagara’s power and decline.

panels.shot: wide cosmopolitan street scene with mixed communities

dialogue.text: Across their different voices runs a common thread: a city of walls and bazaars, temples and armies, where Hindus, Muslims, and foreigners lived and traded in a dense, cosmopolitan world.

panels.shot: tight close-up on overlapping manuscripts in different scripts

dialogue.text: Yet their texts are not simple mirrors. Razzaq’s ornate style, Paes’s trader’s eye, Conti’s memory, Nunes’s compilations—each bends the city slightly to his own purpose and language.

panels.shot: medium shot of a modern historian reading these accounts

dialogue.text: Recent scholars, like those of Textures of Time, warn us: count their elephants and soldiers with care, listen to their wonder, but test every number against archaeology, inscriptions and other eyes.

panels.shot: final wide shot of ruined Hampi with ghostly figures of the four travelers

dialogue.text: Their words still guide us through Vijayanagara’s vanished streets—reminding us to cherish eyewitnesses for their vividness, but to walk beside them with questions, not blind belief.
